Alhamdulillah. continuing in the book of fasting to finish up what we were not able to finish during the last during Ramadan. We are at the close to the end of of the book of fasting. And we were discussing the subchapter regarding those things which are mendup. Those things which are highly recommended, highly encouraged that a person do during his time of fasting while he is fasting or some of these things may even be obligatory. But the reason why the author put them in this category and didn't put them in the conditions, didn't put them in the pillars is because one of these things being compromised doesn't compromise the validity of a person's fast. It may compromise the reward of a person's fast because one of the things that he mentions in this chapter is is refraining from and you know haram speech haram speech haram action. Obviously the this is this is obligatory. This is binding. However, he put it in this category because if a person were to engage in that, it would not invalidate his fasting to the meaning that he wouldn't have to make the day up. Technically, he fasted technically. However, the reward of his fasting may be greatly depleted, severely depleted, completely depleted depending on the severity of the action. And we remember the narration of there are some fasting that the only thing they gain by fasting is hunger. We know this narration. We've shared it a number of times. So what would cause this to be the case? Well, if a person does what is haram or does what invalidates the spirit of fasting even though they technically physically fasted then yeah sure their fast is technically valid meaning in the sense that they are not told to redo it. However, the reward of that fast has been greatly depleted or even entirely depleted to the point where there's no reward at all. And the same thing goes with fah. The same thing goes with the same thing goes with even right. So a person can do the right thing do the act of kindness or do the act of worship but then invalidate the reward for it right in a specific sense and in a general sense. So the last thing that we spoke about in this chapter is that when a person breaks his fast, he breaks his fast on fresh dates or dried dates or water. We mentioned that narration. The narration is in sunb. The final thing that the author mentions in this chapter, that which is encouraged, that which is highly encouraged that a person fasting does while he is fasting, he says that a person break his fast with the recorded dua from that the prophet sallall.aii wasallam was narrated to have made. And then he cites the narration of Abdullahbar who says Omar narrates that the prophet sallall.aii When he would break his fast, he would say the dua. Three sentences. Three sentences. The thirst has been quenched. And the veins have been quenched inshallah. And the reward has been affirmed. Be it the will of Allah. Now there are other narrations that mention other supplications that a person make when he breaks his fast. All of those other narrations are inauthentic. Some of them are not even narrations, right? So the next chapter that the author gives us is what is permissible for the person who is fasting and he mentions a few things which we discussed before. Number one is that a person bathe to cool off and we discussed this a few days ago. Number two is to rinse one's mouth and nose with water without exaggerating to the point where they would swallow the water. And we took a lot of rulings with respect to this. And one of the things of our culture today that falls into this ruling is also using toothpaste. Is it permissible to brush a person for a person to brush one's teeth using toothpaste while he is fasting? The answer is yes. There's no problem with that. As long as they do not as long as they follow the guidelines of what the prophet sallallahu not exaggerate in taking this water or toothpaste or whatever it is to the back of his throat or deep into his nose to where it would go down his throat or her throat. And a lot of people they they find this uncomfortable, you know, brushing your teeth with toothpaste while you're fasting. And that's fine. And what follows this as well is giving blood, donating blood, having blood drawn, having blood drawn for tests. None of this will break a person's fast. However, if a person becomes severely depleted in doing so to where they they need to, you know, they become laded or of course then a person should break his fast. summary: This lecture continues the discussion of the book of fasting, focusing on recommended actions during fasting and what is permissible for those who are fasting. The speaker clarifies the distinction between actions that invalidate a fast versus those that deplete its reward, using the hadith about fasting that yields only hunger. The lecture addresses the dua for breaking fast, warns against fabricated narrations circulating on social media and in decorative materials, and discusses permissible activities such as bathing, rinsing the mouth, brushing teeth with toothpaste, and blood donation. The speaker emphasizes the importance of authentic Islamic knowledge and the difference between what is haram versus merely uncomfortable or inappropriate.
suggested_title: Recommended Acts During Fasting and Permissible Actions for the Fasting Person
hadith_chapter: Book of Fasting - Chapter on Recommended and Permissible Acts
